exercises.title: Inspect live connections
exercises.description: Use ss to inspect TCP state, queues, and PIDs for your active connections.
exercises.expected_outcome: You can identify ESTABLISHED sockets and their owning processes.

exercises.title: Watch a handshake
exercises.description: Capture a TCP handshake to a public host or local server and label SYN, SYN-ACK, and ACK.
exercises.expected_outcome: You see the three handshake packets in order.

exercises.title: Write a TCP echo server
exercises.description: Build a raw-socket echo server and test it with nc.
exercises.expected_outcome: The server accepts a connection and echoes payloads back.

exercises.title: Create many short connections
exercises.description: Open many short-lived connections to a local server and observe TIME_WAIT growth.
exercises.expected_outcome: ss shows many TIME_WAIT sockets after the burst.

exercises.title: Build a tiny HTTP server on TCP
exercises.description: Implement a basic GET-only HTTP/1.1 server with sockets and no framework.
exercises.expected_outcome: A browser or curl can fetch a response from your server.

exercises.title: Benchmark pooling vs no pooling
exercises.description: Write a client benchmark that measures throughput with a fresh TCP connection per request and with pooling.
exercises.expected_outcome: Pooled connections show lower latency and higher throughput.
